title: activity3 report draft
body:
# FINS3666 Assessment 1 Activity 3 - Analysis Report Draft

## 3.1 Data Processing and Microstructure Analysis
I processed four raw datasets covering intraday quotes (1-minute and 1-second), trade prints, and one-year
daily data for PXA across ASX and Cboe venues. I restricted intraday and trade analysis to continuous
trading hours (10:10:00 to before 16:00:00), removed missing and non-positive values, and filtered out
crossed markets where ask was below bid. These steps were important to avoid mechanically biased features
and unrealistic execution assumptions.

From cleaned 1-minute data, I computed standard microstructure metrics including bid-ask spread (dollar and
bps), depth, and order imbalance. I merged minute trade aggregates (count, volume, value, VWAP) and added
rolling features such as return volatility, VWAP deviation, and spread percentile. For 15-Jan-2025, I used
1-second data to derive shock indicators (spread spike z-score, imbalance shock, micro-volatility) and
aggregated them to minute level as an additional market-state filter.

## 3.2 Proprietary Trading Strategy Implementation
I implemented a microstructure mean-reversion strategy on PXA.AX. The core signal was a z-score of
mid-price deviation from rolling 20-minute VWAP. Long entries required sufficiently negative z-score,
controlled imbalance, and acceptable spread regime; short entries mirrored the rule. Exits were triggered by
z-score mean reversion, stop-loss, max holding period, and end-of-day flattening.

Position sizing was volatility-targeted, using rolling annualized 1-minute volatility with leverage clipping.
Execution used next-bar mid-price and conservative costs (4 bps one-way plus 1 bps slippage per position
change). On full sample, the strategy delivered net return -0.4668, annualized
Sharpe -8.8923, and max drawdown -0.4688. A walk-forward
evaluation with rolling recalibration on the out-of-sample date segment produced mean daily net return
-0.00691 with positive-day ratio 0.0000.

## 3.3 Critical Reflection
Strengths include transparent rule design, strict anti-look-ahead implementation, realistic cost treatment,
and explicit out-of-sample validation. The robustness check on PXA.CHA showed net return
-0.4572 and Sharpe -9.1434, which helped assess venue
dependence of alpha quality.

Limitations remain. The strategy is still linear and threshold-based, and may underperform during structural
regime shifts or sparse-liquidity intervals. Cost assumptions are conservative but simplified; they do not
fully model queue position, hidden liquidity, or adverse selection dynamics.

For practical improvement, I would add regime classification, adaptive thresholding, richer event features
(trade qualifiers and auction-state flags), and a more granular execution simulator. Data requirements would
include full depth order book snapshots, queue estimates, and venue-level fee/rebate schedules. Expected
outcomes are improved stability, lower drawdown, and more reliable cross-venue portability.

Implementation governance was included by logging data filters, parameter snapshots, and walk-forward slice diagnostics. This supports reproducibility, transparent peer review, and structured iteration across future strategy versions with consistent evaluation criteria.
